prompt: A chat feature streams responses. Users say it 'starts fast but drags on' for long answers. Which statements correctly explain or address this? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Time-to-first-token is low, but total time grows with output length at the given token rate
Capping or shortening the output would reduce the 'dragging' portion of the latency
Streaming has already minimized total generation time, so output length no longer matters
Switching to a smaller, faster model could raise the tokens-per-second rate
explanation: Streaming lowers time-to-first-token, but total time still scales with output length, so capping output or using a faster model addresses the drag. The wrong option claims streaming makes output length irrelevant, but streaming only changes when tokens appear, not how long the full answer takes.

prompt: A cascade sends every request to a small model first; if a confidence check fails it escalates to a large model. The small model handles 70% of requests; 30% escalate. Per request, the small call costs $0.0001 and the large call costs $0.0020. What is the approximate blended cost per request?
explanation: Every request pays the small call ($0.0001), and 30% also pay the large call (0.30 * $0.0020 = $0.0006), for about $0.00073. The other answers forget the small call is always paid, or count only one path.

prompt: An architect is reviewing why a single user action is far more expensive and slower than the team expected, even though each individual model call looks cheap and fast. Which are plausible root causes worth investigating? (Select all that apply.)
choices: The action fans out into several model calls (retrieval rerank, tool use, final synthesis) per user action
Failed calls are silently retried, multiplying token spend and latency
Per-token price is the only factor and pipeline structure cannot affect cost
Outputs are uncapped, so some responses generate far more tokens than assumed
explanation: Fan-out into many calls, silent retries, and uncapped outputs each multiply the per-action cost and latency even when one call looks cheap. The claim that per-token price is the only factor is false, since pipeline structure is exactly what drives the total here.
